coredata.dc:title: Modification and functional investigation of self-assembled two-dimensional TiO2 nanosheets with abundant hydroxyl groups
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jiao
Deng, Penghao
Wang, Xinyu
Huang, Jifa
Li, Pengkai
coredata.dc:description: Self-assembled two-dimensional (2D) TiO2 nanosheets are only excited under UV light. They suffer from weak visible light absorption and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-holes carriers. In this paper, four kinds of functionalized modifications were realized through complexing self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets with Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe2O3 nanoparticles, N doping, and combining with carbon quantum dots due to the large amount of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the nanosheet, thereby forming the TiO2/Fe3O4, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2, and TiO2/C composites. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the synthesized products, confirming the successful synthesis of self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites. By modification, the increasingly improved photocatalytic properties of self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets are also presented. These findings may open up a new avenue and stimulate further research to prepare more self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-dimensional TiO2
Modification
Surface hydroxyl groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the synthesis procedure of TiO2/Fe3O4 composites; (b–d) SEM images of TiO2/Fe3O4 composites with starting FeCl3⋅6H2O amounts of 0.5 g.

(a–b) Low- and high-resolution TEM images of TiO2/Fe3O4 composites with starting FeCl3⋅6H2O amounts of 0.5 g, TiO2 nanosheets sparsely coated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles, showing natural wrinkles of 2D TiO2 nanosheet; (c) Corresponding XRD pattern and (d) EDS analysis; (e, f) HRTEM images of Fe3O4 nanoparticle and 2D TiO2 nanosheet surface; (g) The behaviour of the composites under an external magnetic field. After 2 min, the black powder dispersed into deionized water was all adsorbed to the side of the magnet.

XPS spectra of the Ti 2p (a) and Fe 2p (b) peak of TiO2/Fe3O4 composites; (c, d) O 1s spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/Fe3O4 composites (corresponding to TiO2 nanosheets before and after solvothermal reaction).

(a) TEM image of TiO2/Fe2O3 composites; (b) HRTEM image of the Fe2O3 nanoparticle surface; (c) HRTEM image of the TiO2 nanosheet surface; (d) XRD pattern of the TiO2/Fe2O3 composites, “T” represents TiO2. The inset in Fig. d is the photograph of the TiO2/Fe2O3 sample.
(a–b) Low- and high-resolution TEM images of N–TiO2 nanosheets; (c) XRD pattern of N–TiO2 nanosheets; (d) XPS spectra of the pure TiO2 and N-doped TiO2 nanosheets, the inset shows the N 1s spectrum.
(a) Absorption spectra evolution of RhB solutions with N-doped TiO2 nanosheets as a function of UV irradiation time; (b) The linear fitting of -ln(C/C0) versus irradiation time.

(a–b) TEM images of different regions of TiO2–C composites, and C nanoparticles can be seen obviously in the red elliptical region; (c) XRD pattern of TiO2–C composites. The inset in Fig. C is the photograph of the TiO2–C sample. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Degradation curves of the RhB aqueous solutions containing different photocatalysts under UV irradiation: pure TiO2 nanosheets, N-doped TiO2 nanosheets and TiO2–C composites; (b) Degradation curves of the RhB aqueous solutions containing different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation: pure TiO2 nanosheets, N-doped TiO2 nanosheets, TiO2/Fe2O3 and TiO2–C composites.
The photocatalytic mechanism over the TiO2/Fe3O4, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2 and TiO2/C nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Modification and functional investigation of self-assembled two-dimensional TiO2 nanosheets with abundant hydroxyl groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Engineering, Henan University of Science and Technology, Luoyang, 471023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physics and Engineering, Henan University of Science and Technology, Luoyang, 471023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-assembled two-dimensional (2D) TiO2 nanosheets are only excited under UV light. They suffer from weak visible light absorption and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-holes carriers. In this paper, four kinds of functionalized modifications were realized through complexing self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets with Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe2O3 nanoparticles, N doping, and combining with carbon quantum dots due to the large amount of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the nanosheet, thereby forming the TiO2/Fe3O4, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2, and TiO2/C composites. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the synthesized products, confirming the successful synthesis of self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites. By modification, the increasingly improved photocatalytic properties of self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets are also presented. These findings may open up a new avenue and stimulate further research to prepare more self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) TiO2 has received more and more attention owing to their unique physical and chemical properties [1–5]. Many studies have confirmed that a 2D nanostructure with ultrathin thickness feature high surface-to-volume ratio, exceptional mechanical strength, intriguing chemical reactivity, as well as other supreme properties [6–11]. Our research work [11] proved that the 2D TiO2 nanosheets-based UV photodetector exhibited an ultralow dark current and ultrahigh Ilight/Idark, corresponding excellent photoresponse sensitivity. Sheng. et al. [7]. demonstrated that the photoanodes made from 2D TiO2 nanosheets showed significantly enhanced efficiency in dye-sensitized solar cells. However, the practical application of 2D TiO2-based energy conversion and storage devices, especially photocatalysts is still limited. One reason is that its large band gap only absorbs UV light in the solar spectrum, and another reason is due to the high recombination rate of the charge carriers. Many modifications have been made by forming composites or hybridizing TiO2 with other materials [12–17], such as metal and non-metal doping, valuable metal deposition, coating carbon quantum dots [18–22] and coupling TiO2 with other semiconductors. However, most of the researches are focused on 0D TiO2 nanoparticles/other 0D nanoparticles [23]/1D nanowires [24]/2D graphene [25] composites or 1D TiO2 nanowires/other 0D noble metal particles [26,27] composites. A small amount of research work on 2D TiO2 nanosheets based composites, especially for self-assembled TiO2 nanosheets in this work, which are different from the exfoliated and facet-controlled TiO2 nanosheets [9,11]. Besides, in the synthesis of these complexes, in order to achieve effective compounding, most of the TiO2 are first pretreated to contain a certain amount of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups, which inevitably make the preparation method complicated and costly.

In our work, it was found that the surface of 2D TiO2 nanosheet obtained by the molecular self-assembly method inherently contain a large amount of hydroxyl functional groups, and the presence of the hydroxyl group will provide favorable condition for the preparation of the 2D TiO2 composite structure. Herein, the self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites (TiO2/Fe3O4, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2, and TiO2/C composites) are synthesized by a polyhydroxy approach. It is worth noting that there is no surface pretreatment to introduce new surface functional groups or additional covalent interconnectivity throughout the entire preparation due to the large amount of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the nanosheet. Additionally, the presence of TiO2 nanosheets can effectively increase specific surface area of catalysts and the layered structure of such a supporting matrix can effectively suppress the agglomeration of nanoparticles as well as facilitate the transfer of the photo-generated electrons to the surface of photocatalysts. Synthesis, formation mechanism and characterizations are analyaed in detail in this paper.
The detail experimental process and Characterization are available in the Supporting Information.

The overall synthesis procedure employed for the preparation of TiO2/Fe3O4 composites is briefly composed of the electrostatic attraction and the solvothermal process as illustrate in Fig. 1 a. The self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets have an extremely high percentage of surfaces and thus contain a large amount of hydroxyl groups (-OH). Assuming that the hydroxyl groups are uniformly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanosheet, it is regarded as the target position of Fe3+ through electrostatic attraction, so that we can speculate that the hydroxyl groups can coordinate the Fe3+ and serve as a nucleation center for the growth of Fe3O4. During the solvothermal reaction, Fe3+ ions are reduced in situ to Fe3O4 and gradually grow into aggregations of particles. This means that in our experiments, no surface pretreatments are required to introduce hydroxyl groups, and TiO2/Fe3O4 composites can be successfully obtained. The morphology of the composites is characterized by SEM and TEM (Fig. 1b–d; Fig. 2 a–b). Wrinkles of TiO2 nanosheets, a characteristic feature of 2D structure, are observed. Both SEM and TEM images show that the individual Fe3O4 nanoparticle are well separated from each other and well spread out on the TiO2 nanosheets. No significant aggregation of Fe3O4 nanoparticle was observed on the TiO2 nanosheets, nor large areas of the TiO2 nanosheets without Fe3O4 decoration. The size of the Fe3O4 nanoparticle is distributed between 70 and 100 nm as shown by the high-resolution SEM and TEM images. From Fig. 1d, which reveals that the spherical nanoparticles are actually aggregations of a great deal of small Fe3O4 particles with an average size of about 20.4 nm. Fig. 2c and Fig. S1 show the XRD patterns of Fe3O4 particles and the TiO2/Fe3O4 composites. All diffraction peaks are consistent with either those of the anatase TiO2 (JCPDS file No. 21-1272) or cubic magnetite Fe3O4 (JCPDS file No. 19-0629). Furthermore, EDS characterization also demonstrates that the composites contain the elements Fe, Ti, and O, thus illustrating the presence of both Fe3O4 and TiO2 (Fig. 2d). From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the composites (Fig. 2e and f), the lattice-fringer spacings of 2.53 Å and 1.90 Å are consistent with the (311) plane of the cubic magnetite Fe3O4 and the (200) plane of anatase crystal structure of TiO2, respectively. It is also concluded that Fe3O4 particles successfully grew on the surfaces of TiO2 nanosheets. The prepared composite was ultrasonically dispersed into deionized water to observe the magnetic separation process. The result is shown in Fig. 2g, it demonstrates that the composites can be easily manipulated by an applied magnetic field, which is very promising in the applications ranging from electromagnetic devices to biomedicine. Especially considering the high specific surface area and good biocompatibility of 2D TiO2, the obtained TiO2/Fe3O4 composites are very suitable for the immobilization and delivery of drugs.

However, the photoactivity testing results showed that the activity of TiO2/Fe3O4 samples was very poor (not shown in this paper). Owing to the direct contact of TiO2 nanosheets and Fe3O4, the photogenerated electrons from TiO2 is thermodynamically feasibly transferred into the lower lying conduction band of Fe3O4 and the holes transferred to the upper lying valence of this Fe3O4, resulting in an increase in the recombination of electron–hole, as shown in Fig. 10a. The lower oxidizing power of the photogenerated holes transferring to the Fe3O4, plus with the induced photodissolution phenomena due to these injected electrons, played a key role in the poor activity. Therefore, TiO2 nanosheets based photocatalysts directly coupled with Fe3O4 particles still face some challenges to improve their photoactivity in our work, it will not be analyzed in the following performance analysis.

The effect of starting Fe3+ ions concentration on the morphology of samples was investigated. Fig. S2 shows the TEM images of the resulting composites with starting FeCl3⋅6H2O amounts of 1.0 and 0.5 g, while keeping the concentration of TiO2 nanosheets invariable. It is clearly demonstrated that the coverage density of Fe3O4 nanoparticles increased with the increase in Fe3+ ions concentration in the precursor mixture. There is also a slight change in the size distribution of nanoparticles. This is certainly due to the increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the precursor solution, and more Fe3O4 nucleation deviates from the hydroxyl position on the surface of TiO2 nanosheets, resulting in the increase of the coverage density of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Thus, the concentration of Fe3+ ions influenced both the coverage density and size of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. This proves that we can rationally adjust the size and density of nanoparticles by regulating the concentration of metal ions.

In order to verify the formation mechanism of the TiO2/Fe3O4 composites, XPS was employed to analyze the chemical information of pure TiO2 nanosheets and the composites. Fig. 3 shows the Ti 2p, Fe 2p and O 1s spectra of as-prepared products, indicating the formation of TiO2 and Fe3O4. Regarding the O 1s peaks, taken on the surface of TiO2 nanosheets before and after solvothermal reaction (Fig. 3c and d), it is observed that three peaks can be fitted. The low-binding-energy peak is ascribed to the Ti–O and Fe–O bonds. The medium binding-energy peak is associated with O2− ions that are in oxygen-deficient regions within the matrix. There are also indications supporting the formation of –OH groups on the surface. The appearance of the high-binding-energy peak indicates the presence of loosely bound oxygen on the surface of nanosheets[11]. For the pure TiO2 nanosheets before solvothermal reaction, the surface contains a large amount of hydroxyl groups due to its large specific surface area. Positive Fe3+ ions can be easily combined with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of nanosheet through electrostatic attraction. Therefore, we demonstrated that the relative intensity of the peak associated with surface hydroxyl groups decreased markedly after solvothermal reaction (the TiO2/Fe3O4 composite has been formed), suggesting that most of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of TiO2 nanosheets have been successfully anchored by metal ions, which is consistent with our mechanism proposed above. It is notable that the peaks of O 1s in TiO2/Fe3O4 composite spectra shifted to higher binding energy, which also suggests possible coordination of Fe element with hydroxyl groups and replacement of hydrogen in hydroxyl groups. The peak at 530.2 eV should be caused by the bond of Fe–O–Ti formed between Fe3O4 and TiO2, suggesting the presence of interfacial interaction (Fe–O–Ti linkage) between Fe3O4 nanoparticles and TiO2.

The morphology and microstructure of the TiO2/Fe2O3 composite was identified by SEM, XRD, TEM and HRTEM. The formation mechanism is similar to the TiO2/Fe3O4 composite. Fig. 4 shows SEM images with different resolution of the as-prepared composite nanostructure. It can be clearly observed that 2D TiO2 nanosheets are uniformly decorated with a large quantity of spherical Fe2O3 nanoparticles (with size of 160–180 nm). Fig. 5 a is the typical TEM image of TiO2/Fe2O3 composite, further confirming the nanoparticles are undoubtedly loaded onto the surface of TiO2 nanosheets. HRTEM images reveal more details concerning the hybrid structure (Fig. 5b and c). The lattice fringe spacing of 2.52 Å nm and 2.37 Å are consistent with the interplanar distances of (110) plane of α-Fe2O3 and (004) plane of TiO2. Fig. 5d shows the XRD pattern of TiO2/Fe2O3 composites. All diffraction peaks could be indexed according to the JCPDS file No. 21-1272 and the JCPDS file No. 33-0664, indicating that the composites mainly consist of the anatase TiO2 phase and the orthorhombic α-Fe2O3 phase. The inset in Fig. 5d is the photograph of the TiO2/Fe2O3 composites, indicating that the TiO2 nanosheets exhibits a reddish brown color after loading Fe2O3 nanoparticles. Energy dispersive X-ray analysis further revealed that only Ti, Fe and O elements were present in the composites (Fig. S4). XPS characterization is carried out to analyze the elemental composition. As shown the inset in Fig. S4, the XPS spectrum of TiO2/Fe2O3 composites shows the presence of the peaks Ti 2p, O 1s, and Fe 2p.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum has been performed to measure the photoresponse of TiO2 nanosheets after loaded Fe2O3 nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. S5. The absorption edge for pure TiO2 nanosheets is approximately 380 nm, which exhibits a weak absorption for visible-light, while the TiO2/Fe2O3 composite shows significant absorption in the region of 380–700 nm. It indicates the effective photoabsorption property of TiO2/Fe2O3 composites in the visible region. The band gap of Fe2O3 was only 2.2 eV. The presence of this narrow band gap semiconductor greatly ameliorated the light absorption properties of the TiO2/Fe2O3 composite (Fig. 10b). Moreover, Fe2O3 interacted with TiO2 nanosheets surface –OH hydroxyl groups, resulting in forming Fe–O–Ti bonds through hydrothermal reaction. The Fe–O–Ti linkage between Fe2O3 nanoparticles and TiO2 nanosheets ensured the strong interfacial interaction and structure stability of TiO2/Fe2O3 composites, resulting in rapid electron transfer. The photocatalytic activity of the TiO2/Fe2O3 composites was measured by monitoring the decoloration of RhB in aqueous solution. Fig. S6 shows the absorbance evolution of RhB solution with TiO2/Fe2O3 catalyst versus visible light irradiation time. The absorbance at 553 nm drops gradually with increasing irradiation time after 240 min, indicating that the TiO2/Fe2O3 composites exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity during the reaction. Therefore, the good visible-light photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the strong absorption in the visible region, the large specific surface area of TiO2 nanosheets and the fast separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs of the Fe2O3 structure.

Fig. 6 a and b illustrates TEM images of N doped TiO2 nanosheets. The characteristics of nanosheets can be clearly distinguished in low- and high-resolution TEM images. Moreover, it can be seen significant wrinkles and stacking of nanosheets, and the size of each N–TiO2 nanosheet is distributed between 100 and 500 nm. The XRD pattern of the as-prepared N doped TiO2 nanosheets is shown in Fig. 6c and compared to the standard card of anatase phase (PDF 21-1272). The diffraction peaks can be readily indexed to the crystal structure of the anatase TiO2. In order to confirm that N atoms were doped into TiO2 nanosheets, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy studies were carried out. Fig. 6 (d) shows the XPS survey spectra of pure and doped TiO2 nanosheets, in which the obvious C 1s, O 1s, and Ti 2p peaks can be clearly detected. The N 1s peak located at a binding energy of 399.2 eV is found in the N-doped TiO2 samples can be attributed to the characteristic peak of the O–Ti–N linkages, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6d, which confirms the N atom is substitutionally doped into the TiO2 crystal lattice.

The absorption spectrum of N-doped TiO2 nanosheets (Fig. S7) shows a shift of the absorption band toward the visible light range. The pure TiO2 nanosheets displayed an absorption band lower than 400 nm due to the charge from the O 2p valence band to the Ti 3d conduction band. The introduction of nitrogen into the lattice of TiO2 alters the electronic structure of TiO2, resulting in the formation of N-dopant levels above the O 2p VB (Fig. 10c). The narrowed bandgap increases the utilization of visible light and promotes the production of photo-induced electrons and holes, leading to a better photocatalytic performance than pure TiO2 nanosheets (Fig. 7 ; Fig. 9b). The photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) using the N-doped TiO2 nanosheets was conducted, pure TiO2-500 nanosheets for comparison. Fig. 7a shows absorption spectra evolution of RhB solutions with N-doped TiO2 nanosheets as a function of irradiation time. The absorption intensity of the RhB solution decreased with UV irradiation time. Under the UV irradiation in a period of 40 min, the degradation efficiency of RhB is about 89% for N–TiO2 sample, which is higher than that for TiO2-500 (about 75% after 40 min, corresponding absorption spectra are not shown here [11]). Finally, the N-doped TiO2 nanosheet is able to degrade about 96.4% of the original organic dye. The apparent rate constant for RhB photodegradation is about 0.0539 min−1 for N-doped TiO2, which is about 1.2 times that of pure TiO2-500 TiO2 (0.0451 min−1). These results imply that N-doped TiO2 is superior to pure TiO2 for RhB photodegradation due to the high production of photo-induced electrons and holes.

Next, further improving the photocatalytic performance of self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets under ultraviolet light has been achieved. TEM images (Fig. 8 a and b) show that the TiO2–C composites consisted of ultrathin 2D TiO2 nanosheets decorated with carbon quantum dots. Wrinkled TiO2 nanosheets are observed, and carbon quantum dot (about 4–6 nm in size) are uniformly distributed on TiO2 nanosheets. The good distribution of carbon quantum dots on TiO2 sheets guarantees the efficient photocatalytic properties of TiO2–C composites. Fig. 8c shows XRD pattern of the TiO2–C composites in which anatase is the major phase and rutile is the minor phase. The absence of graphite peaks indicates that the carbon is in an amorphous state. The photograph of the TiO2–C composites in Fig. 8c shows that the products are black powder after loading carbon quantum dots.
The photocatalytic degradation of RhB using the TiO2–C composites was conducted, together with those on pure TiO2 and N-doped TiO2 nanosheets for comparison under the same conditions.

Fig. 9 a shows the photo-degradation of RhB under UV irradiation for various photocatalysts. As compared with TiO2 and N-doped TiO2 photocatalysts, TiO2–C catalysts show a much higher UV light photocatalytic activity in the degradation of RhB. It can be seen that the degradation efficiency is as high as 90% after only 10 min UV irradiation. After 30 min, the TiO2–C catalyst is able to degrade to 100% of the original dye, far ahead of P25 and other two catalysts in this work. A photocatalytic mechanism over TiO2–C catalysts is proposed in Fig. 10 d. When UV light irradiates the surface of TiO2–C nanoparticles, the carbon on the surface can also enhance the light absorption and reduce the reflection of light as it acts as a sensitizer. Moreover, the carbon on the surface takes up the electrons, promoting the separation of the electrons and holes and keeping them highly reactive. It is only in this situation that surface bound water molecules are converted to the OH· radicals, which then subsequently photodegrade dye, while the carbon is protected by the previous electron transfer. Our study demonstrates that the structural combination of 2D TiO2 nanosheets and carbon quantum dots leads to highest UV light photocatalytic activity.

Finally, the photocatalytic properties of bare TiO2, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2, and TiO2/C composites was also compared under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 9b). The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by monitoring the decoloration of RhB in aqueous solution. When bare TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/Fe2O3, and N-doped TiO2 photocatalysts were employed, the decoloration rate of organic dye RhB after 180 min was about 13%, 32%, 55% and 98% respectively.

We demonstrated that the self-assembled 2D TiO2 nanosheets-based composites: such as TiO2/Fe3O4, TiO2/Fe2O3, N-doped TiO2, and TiO2/C, can be fabricated through an efficient polyhydroxy method without any surface pretreatment. The success of preparation for self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites was confirmed by the results of XRD, SEM, TEM, HRTEM and EDS. These results show that the individual Fe3O4, or Fe2O3 nanoparticles, or carbon quantum dots are well separated from each other and well spread out on the 2D TiO2 nanosheets. The success of N doping into the lattice of 2D TiO2 was verified by XPS and UV–vis absorption spectra. Comparing with pure self-assembled TiO2 nanosheets, the TiO2/Fe3O4 composites exhibit a rapid magnetic separation by added an external magnetic field, the TiO2/Fe2O3 composites show effective photo-absorption property in the visible region; the N-doped TiO2 nanosheets possess superior photocatalytic performance for RhB degradation due to the high production of photo-induced electrons and holes; but the highest photocatalytic efficiency is the TiO2/carbon quantum dots catalysts, which is far ahead. In general, our work provides a new thought to design and prepare other self-assembled 2D TiO2-based composites semiconductors for various applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402090) from Henan University of Science and Technology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold–palladium bimetallic nanoalloy decorated ultrathin 2D TiO2 nanosheets as efficient photocatalysts with high hydrogen evolution activity
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xin Y, Wu L, Lei G, et al. Gold-palladium bimetallic nanoalloy decorated ultrathin 2D TiO2 nanosheets as efficient photocatalysts with high hydrogen evolution activity [J]. Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 2015, 3(16):8659-8666.
